strands.title: Replay Module Requirements & Design Intent
strands.content: The replay module (src/pichay/replay.py) must reconstruct offline simulations of context paging behavior from proxy JSONL logs. Core purpose: enable post-hoc evaluation of paging effectiveness by replaying exact message array states at each API call turn, applying compaction logic to detect page faults (model re-requesting evicted content), and computing aggregate statistics (fault rate, bytes saved, reduction percentage). This bridges proxy logs to pager compaction logic. User mandate: 'Read the existing pager.py, proxy.py, and eval.py thoroughly before writing any code. Match the coding style and patterns.' The module must have a CLI entry point with argparse supporting --age-threshold, --min-size, and --json flags. Page faults occur when model's next action re-requests content that was evicted by prior paging operations. The module must provide per-session and aggregate statistics.

key_claims.text: The replay module must parse proxy JSONL logs to reconstruct paging scenarios
The module must reconstruct the messages array as it would have existed at each API call turn
Page faults occur when the model's actual next action re-requests content that was evicted
The module must provide per-session and aggregate statistics (fault rate, bytes saved, reduction %)
A CLI entry point with argparse is required with --age-threshold, --min-size, and --json flags

strands.title: Code Context & Existing Patterns — Partial Knowledge
strands.content: The codebase contains at least four key modules: (1) proxy.py — logs request/response metrics, implements context window paging as 'intervention layer for phase 1 context utilization experiment' sitting between client and API calls; (2) pager.py — context window pager that evicts stale tool results from the messages array; (3) eval.py — evaluation framework that parses proxy JSONL logs from experimental runs and computes per-turn and cumulative metrics; (4) main.py or runner (inferred from turn 10) — single entry point that starts the proxy, launches Claude Code, captures artifacts. The assistant has initiated three separate read operations (turns 6-7, 8-9, 10-11) seeking full file content, but tool results continue to be truncated to opening lines only. This prevents complete understanding of JSONL schema, pager API, and metric computation patterns. The coding style mandate requires matching existing patterns exactly.

key_claims.text: pager.py is titled 'Context window pager' and evicts stale tool results from messages array
eval.py is an evaluation framework that parses JSONL logs and computes per-turn and cumulative metrics
proxy.py implements an intervention layer for context paging sitting between client and API calls
The pager.py compaction logic can be applied to reconstructed message snapshots for fault detection

strands.title: Context Reading Limitation & Information Bottleneck
strands.content: Three read operations (turns 6-7, 8-9, 10-11) have been attempted, each returning only the first few lines of the target files (proxy.py, eval.py, runner). The tool results show opening docstrings and context only (1-5 lines each), not the full file content needed for implementation. This is a hard context constraint: the assistant cannot proceed to implementation without either (a) receiving full file content in a single retrieval, (b) receiving content in structured chunks with line number ranges, or (c) having the user provide a summary of the critical APIs and data structures. The current state represents a read-stall: the cognitive pipeline has initiated correct queries but hit a practical constraint on response size. Without visible error messages from the read operations, the cause is unclear — it could be file length, tool output truncation, or incomplete tool result transmission.

key_claims.text: Three read operations have been initiated but each returned only 1-5 lines of file content
Full JSONL schema, pager API signatures, and metric computation details remain unknown
The read-stall represents a practical constraint, not a logical blocker

strands.title: Critical Implementation Unknowns — Persisting Across Cycles
strands.content: The following remain unresolved and essential for implementation: (1) JSONL log schema — exact field names, structure of messages array representation, timestamp formats, API call metadata; (2) pager.py API — function signatures (parameters, return types), how compaction is invoked, what data structures are modified; (3) Page fault detection — operational definition (e.g., does a fault occur when a message ID in the model's next request appears in the evicted set from a prior compaction?); (4) Metrics calculation — how 'bytes saved', 'reduction percentage', and 'fault rate' are derived from paging operations; (5) Message array reconstruction — whether full turn-by-turn reconstruction is required or if snapshot-based analysis suffices; (6) eval.py patterns — what statistics it computes and whether replay should follow its methodology. These unknowns directly block writing coherent replay.py code.

key_claims.text: The JSONL log format and available fields for reconstruction is not yet known
The pager.py API (function signatures, parameters, return types) needs to be understood
The operational definition of 'page fault' (how to detect re-requested evicted content) is unclear
How 'bytes saved' and 'reduction percentage' are calculated from paging operations is unknown

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Complete source code for proxy.py, pager.py, eval.py, and main.py/runner
declared_losses.why: Tool results from three consecutive read operations (turns 6-7, 8-9, 10-11) returned only 1-5 opening lines of each file. The content needed for implementation (JSONL schema, pager API, metric computation logic) was not transmitted. This is a context_pressure constraint—file content was truncated at retrieval boundary.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Concrete data structure specifications: JSONL field names, pager function signatures, message array format at API calls
declared_losses.why: Without full source files, specific naming conventions, parameter types, return values, and data shapes cannot be extracted. Opening docstrings alone are insufficient for faithful implementation. This is essential semantic content for the next cycle.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Implementation pattern examples from eval.py: how metrics are computed, how logs are parsed, how statistics are aggregated
declared_losses.why: The eval.py opening lines mention 'parses proxy JSONL logs' and 'computes per-turn and cumulative metrics' but the actual implementation details that should guide replay.py design were not included in the truncated output.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Clarity on whether tool/artifact output truncation is a display limitation or a true transmission constraint
declared_losses.why: Three sequential read attempts returned extremely brief outputs. The cause could be tool configuration, response size limits, or incomplete results—but without error signals or explicit truncation markers, the nature of the constraint is ambiguous. This affects how the next retrieval should be structured.

open_questions: What is the complete JSONL schema used by proxy.py logs? Specifically: what fields represent the messages array, API call parameters, model responses, paging operations, and eviction records?
What is the full API of pager.py? What are the exported functions/classes, their exact signatures, parameters, return types, and side effects?
How is 'page fault' operationally detected in the replay context? Does it mean a message requested by the model in turn N+1 was evicted in turn N?
What metrics does eval.py compute, and should replay.py follow its calculation patterns or introduce new ones?
How should 'bytes saved', 'fault rate', and 'reduction percentage' be calculated from paging operations across a session?
Should replay.py reconstruct the full conversation turn-by-turn, maintaining conversation history, or analyze paging snapshots independently?
What is the coding style, naming convention (snake_case vs camelCase), error handling pattern, and docstring format used in the existing codebase?
Are there constraints on how much of pager.py's logic can be reused vs. reimplemented in replay.py? Should replay create its own pager instances or call existing functions?
Should replay.py handle multi-session aggregation, or focus on per-session analysis with a wrapper for aggregation?
